---
title: "Week 9: SAR & Temperature"
---

## Summary

This week we looked closely at SAR data and ways to analyse temperature data using GEE during our practical.

### SAR data and polarisation

SAR data is requires active sensors which emit waves and get 'backscatter' information which can be used to capture things like surface texture.

Commonly Band C SAR is used which has a frequency of 4-8GHz. It can be used in many ways, for example in CASA0025 we've looked at SAR used in building damage detection.

Other bands such as band X which has a frequency of 8-12GHz are higher resolution and can be used for monitoring urban areas, looking at things like change detection.

The electromagnetic waves transmitted can also come in two directions: vertical or horizontal. This is also called polarisaiton.

[![SAR Polarisation (Source: [University of Alaska](https://hyp3-docs.asf.alaska.edu/guides/introduction_to_sar/))](https://hyp3-docs.asf.alaska.edu/images/polarizations_ASF_dashed.png)](https://hyp3-docs.asf.alaska.edu/guides/introduction_to_sar/)

When using SAR data the surface it is emitting EM waves to makes a large difference in how much backscatter will be returned. When sending EM waves over a smooth surface there is limited backscatter return, which can be useful for capturing information for smooth surfaces like water or ice.

![Source: [University of Alaska](https://hyp3-docs.asf.alaska.edu/guides/introduction_to_sar/)](https://hyp3-docs.asf.alaska.edu/images/wavelength_vs_roughness.png)

-   SAR data and polarisation

    -   discuss limitations

-   amplitude and phase

    -   useful for change

-   using Landsat and MODIS for temperature

## Applications

-   compare 2 articles

## Reflections

-   Image fusion interest

-   insar data limitations
